---
title: "Fringed Nutrush (Scleria ciliata)"
slug: "scleria-ciliata"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/625129011/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Fringed Nutrush"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Liliopsida"
tax_order: "Poales"
tax_family: "Cyperaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Liliopsida", "Poales", "Cyperaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/625129011/medium.jpg" alt="Fringed Nutrush" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/dsandii">
dsandii</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/343205382">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Liliopsida
-   **Order:** Poales
-   **Family:** Cyperaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=168654)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Rare species in Florida found in moist to wet habitats such as flatwoods, bogs, and savannas.

## Natural history notes

A grass-like wetland plant characterized by its slender stems, narrow leaves, and small nut-like fruits often edged with fine, fringe-like hairs. In Florida, it is found in moist to wet habitats such as flatwoods, bogs, and savannas, typically in sandy soils. It grows during the warmer months and contributes to plant diversity and structure in wetland communities.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/168654-Scleria-ciliata)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/plant/species/2076)
